questions.question: What is the 'occurs check' in the unification algorithm and why is it necessary? (3 marks)
expectedPoints: Definition: A check to see if a variable appears inside the term it is to be unified with.
Purpose: To prevent infinite and invalid substitutions (e.g., x = f(f(f(...x...))).
Consequence of omission: Without it, the algorithm might create cyclical structures and enter an infinite loop or produce incorrect results.

questions.question: Explain the process of unification with a suitable example. Show each step of the algorithm for unifying P(f(a), g(x)) and P(y, g(b)). (5 marks)
expectedPoints: Initial expressions: P(f(a), g(x)) and P(y, g(b)).
Step 1: Unify P and P -> success. Proceed to arguments.
Step 2: Unify first arguments: f(a) and y. y is a variable. Check: y not in f(a). Substitution θ1 = {y/f(a)}. Apply substitution: Expressions become P(f(a), g(x)) and P(f(a), g(b)).
Step 3: Unify second arguments: g(x) and g(b). Function symbols match.
Step 4: Unify arguments of g: x and b. x is a variable. Check: x not in b. Substitution θ2 = {x/b}.
Final MGU: {y/f(a), x/b}. Unified expression: P(f(a), g(b)).
